results.possibleIdentity: Robert Adler (1913–2007) was a physicist and engineer who is primarily known for co-inventing the wireless television remote control at Zenith Electronics. However, he is historically associated with the Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) due to his technical collaborations with prominent avant-garde artists. In the late 1960s and early 1970s, Adler submitted proposals to E.A.T. for the Pepsi Pavilion at Expo '70 in Osaka, Japan. He worked alongside artists and composers such as La Monte Young, Marian Zazeela, and Dennis Johnson to develop and program light and sound systems. His participation in E.A.T. exemplified the organization's mission to bridge the gap between engineering and artistic practice.

Robert Adler (born 1930) is an American painter who was active in the New York art scene during the 1960s and 1970s. His work, which often included oil landscapes such as "Corn Hill," was represented by the Poindexter Gallery in New York City. His paintings were featured in exhibitions during this period, including the "A Sense of Place: The Artist and the American Land" exhibition, which highlighted contemporary American artists' relationship with the environment.

Richard Adler (1907–1977) was a German artist, etcher, and painter active in the mid-20th century who frequently signed his works as "R. Adler." Based in Berlin and Düsseldorf, he was known for his cityscapes and architectural etchings of locations such as Hamburg, Lübeck, and Berlin. His work remained active in the European art market and exhibitions through the 1960s and early 1970s until his death in 1977.

results.possibleIdentity: John Hassell (often known as Jon Hassell, 1937–2021) was an American trumpeter, composer, and experimental artist active in the late 1960s and early 1970s. He is closely associated with the New York avant-garde and the Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) organization. Hassell participated in the landmark E.A.T. exhibition titled 'Some More Beginnings' at the Brooklyn Museum (1968–1969), where he presented a work involving technical materials and processes. He is renowned for developing the 'Fourth World' musical style, which blended traditional ethnic sounds with modern electronic technology. Hassell was also a prominent figure in the New York City 'loft' scene of the late 20th century, often living and working in loft spaces that served as hubs for artistic and technical collaboration. Some historical records and reports, such as those from the National Endowment for the Arts, list him as 'John Hassel' (with one 'l') and mention him in proximity to organizations like 'Loft, Inc.,' which may account for the compound name 'Hassel-Loft.'

results.possibleIdentity: Larry J. Solomon is a composer, music theorist, and educator active since the late 1960s, known for his work in experimental music and the intersection of art, technology, and mathematics. He is a prominent scholar on the works of John Cage and David Tudor, both of whom were key figures in the Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) movement. Solomon's theoretical writings, such as "The Sounds of Silence: John Cage and 4'33"" (first published in the late 1990s but reflecting decades of study), are frequently cited in research concerning the historical E.A.T. performances and the development of indeterminate music. In 1974, his work was included in "The Aspen Papers," a collection from the International Design Conference in Aspen (IDCA), where he appeared alongside other avant-garde figures like Nam June Paik and Gene Youngblood. As a composer, he has explored live electronic music, fractal music, and symmetry as a compositional determinant. He holds a PhD in music and served as a professor at Pima Community College in Tucson, Arizona, for several decades beginning in the early 1970s.

results.possibleIdentity: Jill Rosenberg (now known as Jill Rosenberg Jones) is an arts administrator and collector who was active in the professional and creative circles of New York and Boston in the late 1960s and early 1970s. She is significantly associated with Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.), a non-profit organization that fostered collaboration between artists and engineers. During the late 1960s, she worked at WGBH, the public television station in Boston, which was a major hub for art-technology projects such as the landmark 1969 broadcast 'The Medium is the Medium.' Rosenberg has also been associated with the documentation of experimental performances and served as a film editor for projects related to E.A.T., including films documenting the 1966 '9 Evenings: Theatre and Engineering.' Later in her career, she held senior positions in financial services at institutions like Goldman Sachs and Morgan Stanley. Today, as Jill Rosenberg Jones, she is the Executor of the James Weldon Johnson Literary Estate and Chairperson of the James Weldon Johnson Foundation, where she manages the legacy of the African American artist and civil rights leader. She is also a passionate collector of Australian Aboriginal art.
